coredata.dc:title: Engineering of graphene quantum dots by varying the properties of graphene oxide for fluorescence detection of picric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Mukherjee, Debarati
Das, Pradip
Kundu, Sukanya
Mandal, Bishnupada
coredata.dc:description: The study examines the effect of different forms of graphene oxide (GO) on the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQD). GO synthesized at various temperatures i.e. 30, 50, 110 °C possessed different structural and functional properties and was used as a substrate for GQD preparation. Thorough characterization of the GQDs in terms of their structural, morphological, functional, and optical properties was performed. The GQDs exhibited variation in their size and fluorescence properties depending upon the type of GO used. Hydrothermal reduction of GO, prepared at an oxidation temperature of 50 °C (GO-50), minimized the particle size (3.6 nm) and maximized the photoluminescence (PL) intensity and quantum yield (64.8%) of the GQD (GQD-50). GQD-50 was found to detect picric acid (PA) in an aqueous solution via ‘turn-off’ fluorescence quenching, unlike the other GQDs where the initial precursor is synthesized at 30, 110 °C. Experimental studies summarize that interaction between the fluorophore-quencher resulted in static quenching. The limit of detection was estimated to be 1.2 μM with a detection range of 0–200 μM. The work concludes that optimization of the substrate i.e. GO can result in the development of a simple, non-toxic, cost-effective GQD based sensor for PA detection. The study eliminates the need for doping/functionalization of GQDs as reported previously, and hence finds a promising impact on the development of sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Graphene quantum dot
Fluorescence quenching
Picric acid
Static quenching

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Improved Carbon-substrate nanomaterials for treatment and detection of hazardous pollutants in the water/wastewater Edited by Alireza Khataee, Yasser Vasseghian, and Amir Razmjou

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of highly functionalized graphene oxide (GO)
Preparation of high fluorescence graphene quantum dots (GQD)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The characterization facilities provided by the facility of the central instrument (CIF), Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Guwahati are kindly acknowledged by the authors. The authors are thankful for the assistance with the fluorescence spectrophotometer provided by the Centre for Environment, IIT Guwahati.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of GQD synthesis from GO synthesized at 30, 50, 110 °C and images of aqueous solution of the corresponding GQD (0.5 mg/ml).
TEM micrograph of (a) GO-30 (b) GO-50 (c) GO-110 (d) GQD-30, (e) GQD-50 and (f) GQD-110 samples and its corresponding SAED pattern; AFM images of synthesized (g) GQD-30, (h) GQD-50, (i) GQD-110.
(a) XRD, (b) FTIR and (c) RAMAN spectra of GQD-30, GQD-50, GQD-110.
XPS spectra: (a–c) High-resolution C1s; (d–f) O1s deconvoluted peaks of synthesized GQD-30.
(a) UV spectra and (b–d) PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelength for GQD-30, GQD-50, GQD-110.
TRPL spectra of (a) GQD-30 (b) GQD-50 (c) GQD-110.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of GQD-50 upon addition of different concentrations of PA (excitation wavelength 320 nm) (b) Modified Stern-Volmer plot at different PA concentrations (blue line represents modified S–V fit). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Engineering of graphene quantum dots by varying the properties of graphene oxide for fluorescence detection of picric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Separation Science Laboratory, Department of Chemical Engineering, India Institute of Technology Guwahati, Guwahati, 781039, Assam, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Separation Science Laboratory, Department of Chemical Engineering, India Institute of Technology Guwahati, Guwahati, 781039, Assam, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both the authors contribute equally as first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study examines the effect of different forms of graphene oxide (GO) on the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQD). GO synthesized at various temperatures i.e. 30, 50, 110 °C possessed different structural and functional properties and was used as a substrate for GQD preparation. Thorough characterization of the GQDs in terms of their structural, morphological, functional, and optical properties was performed. The GQDs exhibited variation in their size and fluorescence properties depending upon the type of GO used. Hydrothermal reduction of GO, prepared at an oxidation temperature of 50 °C (GO-50), minimized the particle size (3.6 nm) and maximized the photoluminescence (PL) intensity and quantum yield (64.8%) of the GQD (GQD-50). GQD-50 was found to detect picric acid (PA) in an aqueous solution via ‘turn-off’ fluorescence quenching, unlike the other GQDs where the initial precursor is synthesized at 30, 110 °C. Experimental studies summarize that interaction between the fluorophore-quencher resulted in static quenching. The limit of detection was estimated to be 1.2 μM with a detection range of 0–200 μM. The work concludes that optimization of the substrate i.e. GO can result in the development of a simple, non-toxic, cost-effective GQD based sensor for PA detection. The study eliminates the need for doping/functionalization of GQDs as reported previously, and hence finds a promising impact on the development of sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots are a special group of nanomaterials whose particle size is less than 10 nm. Their size-dependant morphological, optical, electronic, mechanical properties which are different from their bulk forms, have engrossed research curiosity (Di et al., 2018; Radisavljevic et al., 2011). Till date, research efforts are being pursued to explore strategies for synthesis, modification, and applications of semiconductors and carbon based quantum dots (Ji et al., 2014, 2015, 2019; Vatanpour et al., 2022). Among the carbon-based nanomaterials, graphene occupies a special focus as its bandgap is tunable by converting 2-dimensional sheets into zero-dimensional quantum dots (GQDs) (Ritter and Lyding, 2009). Graphene-based composites are being extensively researched to achieve energy as well as environmental sustainability in diverse areas, such as catalysis (Rostamnia et al., 2015), water treatment (Sadeghi Rad et al., 2021), and sensing (Şenocak et al., 2020). GQDs have emerged as a promising new generation material that exhibited superior performance in a wide range of applications including bioimaging, sensors, photovoltaics, a memory device, electrochromic, etc. (Ji et al., 2014, 2015, 2019; Peng et al., 2012). GQDs are smaller fragments of reduced graphene oxide possessing up to ten graphene sheets and size less than 30 nm (Salehnia et al., 2017). The edge effects, structure and quantum confinement due to the size of GQDs results in their fluorescent properties (Salehnia et al., 2017). GQDs, being in line with carbon based materials, are highly photostable, biocompatible, non-toxic which makes them superior compared to other fluorescent probes and quantum dots of semiconductors (Ji et al., 2014; Karatas et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2012; Salehnia et al., 2017).

The variation of size, shape, doping, degree of surface oxidation and functionalization are found to affect the fluorescence properties of GQDs (Ji et al., 2014). Studies have shown that GQD synthesis method or post-synthesis modification strategies are responsible for variation in the aforementioned properties (Chen et al., 2012; Dervishi et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2010a, 2010b). GQDs with varied sizes and properties were synthesized by the bottom-up technique (Dervishi et al., 2019). Various carbon-containing precursors, such as citric acid, carbon black have been investigated for GQD preparation (Khodabakhhshi et al., 2021; Ogi et al., 2014). The present research trend involves assessing the efficiency of GQDs, either nascent or post functionalization, as a sensor for detection of plethora of components, such as cations, anions, organics (e.g., glucose, hydroquinone, and phenols) (Diao et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019). The resonance light scattering method was used for detection of phenolic contents in industrial wastewater using GQD (Sun et al., 2014). 4-Nitro phenol was determined via GQD functionalized with polymer (Zhou et al., 2014). The effect of doping GQDs with nitrogen, sulphur, GO, noble metals, semiconductors to increase their fluorescence quenching has also been reported (Li et al., 2019). Functionalized GQDs, prepared from carbon sources, such as citric acid, have been reported for sensing of picric acid (PA) in aqueous solution (Fan et al., 2021; Kadian and Manik, 2020). GQDs doped with various heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulphur, phosphorous, boron exhibited enhanced fluorescence and contaminant detection efficiency. For instance, GQD has been frequently modified by incorporating nitrogen (N), which resulted in selective detection of PA in natural surface water synthesized via pyrolysis of tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane and citric acid. Fan et al. reported four-fold increased quantum yield and subsequent quenching upon PA addition for Schiff base-functionalized GQDs compared to nitrogen doped GQDs (Fan et al., 2021).
However, the processes are multi-step and hence time-consuming. This calls for a simple method for the synthesis of GQD sensor.

PA or 2,4,6,-trinitrophenol is a widely used explosive with high detonation velocity. This nitroaromatic derivative is used for preparing various dyes, fireworks, pigments, pesticides, and rocket fuel. (Shanmugaraju et al., 2011; Singha et al., 2014). Additionally, their disposal in the environment leads to contamination of soil and water due to high aqueous solubility (Kaja et al., 2020; Takahashi et al., 2004; Wollin and Dieter, 2005). Their toxic effects are more pronounced than even trinitrotoluene (TNT). The intake of PA, even in trace amounts, can cause serious damage to skin, lungs, eyes, kidneys, etc., and adversely affects the normal functioning of the human system (Fan et al., 2021). Prolonged exposure to PA might result in some deadly diseases like cancer, anaemia, and cyanosis. The water health standard (GB5749-2006) of China has approved that the concentration of PA in drinking water should be less than 0.5 mg/L with a permissible limit of 1–37 μg (kg day)−1 for routine consumption (Li et al., 2018; Nagarkar et al., 2016). Hence, their identification is important based on environmental perspectives. Among the detection methods, typically used such as Raman spectroscopy, electrochemical analysis, mass spectrometry, etc. (Ding and Fang, 2010; Shi et al., 2016), fluorescence sensing takes the forefront due to their cost-effectiveness and simplicity.

The present study explores the effect of varying the substrate possessing different structural and functional properties on GQD formation. GO synthesized from graphite at various temperatures i.e. 30, 50, 110 °C were found to possess varied surface functionalities (Das et al., 2020). GO with varied properties were used as the starting material for GQD synthesis and its effect on their size, morphology, functionality, luminescent properties were investigated. Although, modifications in the GQD synthesis process have been widely reported, however, the effect of oxidation temperature dependant GO as substrate have been rarely studied, which depicts the uniqueness of the current work. The efficiency of nascent, self-passivated, photoluminescent as-synthesized GQDs has been assessed for the fluorescent detection of PA, in terms of sensitivity and selectivity. GQD has been used for ‘turn-off’ fluorescence sensing of PA based on host-guest interactions. The quenching mechanism was analysed and the limit of detection was calculated to study the effectiveness of the process. The PA sensing was unaffected by the presence of other aromatics in solution, concluding the GQD sensors are selective in nature. The study entails a protocol to eliminate the additional step of surface activation/modification required for synthesis of GQD sensors, unlike most of the reported works. The present study leverages the ease of foothold of GQD sensors for commercial applications by honing the process simplicity.

The raw material required as the primary source of GQD is graphite powder (Sigma-Aldrich). The synthesis also involved the use of sulphuric acid (H2SO4, analytical grade 98 wt%, Merck), potassium permanganate (KMnO4, Merck), orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4, 88 wt%, Merck), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%, Merck), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 wt%, Merck), sodium hydroxide (NaOH pellets, Merck), which were purchased and used without further purification. The sensing application involved the use of trinitrophenol (TNP, Loba Chemicals). Millipore® (Merck) deionized water was used throughout the experimental processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of highly functionalized graphene oxide (GO)
ce:section.ce:para: The study performed by Das et al. (2020) anticipated a relationship between the synthesis temperature of GO with the incorporation of oxygen-containing functional groups. Accordingly, GO was synthesized at temperatures 30, 50, and 100 °C following the reported protocol. The process involved the oxidation of graphite flakes using a mixture of H2SO4/H3PO4. This was followed by the addition of KMnO4 and continuing the reaction at different oxidation temperatures (i.e. 30, 50, and 100 °C). The reaction mixture was then diluted with water and the addition of H2O2 which changes the colour to yellow indicating the complete reduction of the added KMnO4. Successive washing by acid (0.1 (M) HCl) and deionized water by centrifugation for neutralization followed by vacuum drying (60 °C) resulted in the formation of GO sheets. GO prepared at temperatures 30, 50, 100 °C will henceforth be referred to as GO-30, GO-50, and GO-100, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of high fluorescence graphene quantum dots (GQD)
ce:section.ce:para: GO synthesized at various temperatures was used as the source for GQD. The schematic of GQD synthesis has been represented in Fig. 1 . The synthesis involved hydrothermal reaction following the reported protocol of Qu et al. (2016) with some modifications. GO was dispersed in water (0.5 mg/mL) via ultrasonication for 1 h to exfoliate the stacked sheets. A reducing agent i.e. 0.1 (M) NaOH was added to the GO solution followed by a hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 24 h in a Teflon lined autoclave. The resulting mixture was subjected to a two-step separation process i.e. (i) centrifugation at 1000 rpm to separate the suspended particles and (ii) filtration using 0.22 μm filter paper to obtain the nanometre-sized particles. The GQDs prepared using GO-30, GO-50, and GO-100 as the substrates were denoted as GQD-30, GQD-50, and GQD-100, respectively. The sodium ions were removed by performing dialysis for two times (using 1 kDa molecular weight cut-off dialysis bag), each cycle being 24 h at a stretch. The GQD solution obtained with different substrates have been represented in Fig. 1. The apparent visual difference in the solution colour varying in shades of yellow anticipates a difference in their optical properties. Freeze drying was performed to obtain the solid form of the powders which was further characterized.

The as-synthesized GQDs were characterized in terms of their structural, chemical, morphological, and optical properties. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker, D8 Advance) was performed using Cu Kα radiation (power 40 kV, 40 mA) with 2θ ranging from 3 to 80°, to confirm the formation of graphene structure and assess the crystallinity. The functional groups were judged using Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR, PerkinElmer, Spectrum Version 10.4.3) in attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Fisher Scientific) analysis performed with Mg-Kα and Al-Kα as the source at 15 kV indicated the elemental compositions of the samples. Structural estimation of GQDs was also analysed using Raman spectroscopy (Kaiser Optics, λ = 514 nm). The surface morphology and particle size of the GQDs were analysed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100 F) operated with 200 kV accelerating voltage. The sample preparation involved dispersion in ethanol and drop-casting on an amorphous carbon coated copper grid followed by drying. The topography, sheet size and surface roughness were studied using atomic force microscopy (Cypher, Oxford) in contact mode. The measurement of optical properties involving absorbance and fluorescence were performed using UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Singapore) and spectrofluorometer (AFM, LS 45, PerkinElmer, Singapore), respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of the quantum dots was measured using a picosecond time-resolved fluorometer (Eddinburg Instruments, FSP920).

The quantum yield of the as-synthesized GQDs was measured according to the previously reported protocol (Sun et al., 2013). Quinine sulphate (QS), prepared by dissolving it in 0.1 (M) sulphuric acid, has been used as the typical reference standard. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra for the GQDs and quinine sulphate were recorded at the same excitation wavelength. The quantum yield value was given according to the following equation (eq (1)).
(1)
Q
Y s
= Q
Y r
×
A s
A R
×
A b
s R
A b
s s
×
η S 2
η R 2
Where the quantum yield of the sample and reference are denoted by
Q
Y s
and
Q
Y r
respectively, and
Q
Y r
for quinine sulphate is 54%; area under the PL curve produced at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm and 2 nm slit width for the sample and reference are represented by
A s
and
A R
;
A b
s s
and
A b
s R
are the UV absorbance of the sample and reference, respectively. The solution concentration taken while recording the PL spectra must be the same as that of the UV spectra and in the present case, it was 0.5 mg/ml. The refractive index (η) for both the sample and reference was taken as 1.33.

Various concentrations of PA (10, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200 μM) in deionized water were prepared. For detection of PA, 200 μL PA were added to 1.8 ml of GQD solution (GQD solution concentration: 0.5 mg/ml) (Kaja et al., 2020). The fluorescence of the solution was measured at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm to assess the quenching of GQDs by the non-fluorophore. The experimental conditions involved the natural pH of the solution (pH = 7) and ambient temperature. The fluorescence lifetime of the quenched solution was analysed to determine the nature of quenching i.e. static or dynamic.

TEM images of GO synthesized at 30, 50, and 110 °C are represented in Fig. 2 a–c, respectively. The presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxide groups results in wrinkled morphology (Das et al., 2020). The images (Fig. 2d–f) show uniform spherical morphology of GQDs synthesized with the various GOs. The average size of the GQDs as analysed from their size distribution (Fig. S1) were centred at 4.7, 3.6, and 6.1 nm for GQD-30, GQD-50, GQD-110, respectively (Deng et al., 2015). The high crystalline nature is well reflected by their TEM (SAED) images. The lattice space of GQD-50 has been calculated from their HRTEM image (Fig. S2) using ImageJ software. GQD-50 exhibited a crossed lattice pattern. Interlayer spacing of 0.329 nm corresponds to (002) plane, whereas 0.241 nm corresponds to lattice fringe (1120). The findings are similar with Tachi et al. indicating presence of graphene-stacked structure (Tachi et al., 2019). The spherical morphology of GQD has also been confirmed from their FESEM images (Fig. S3).

AFM images depict the uniform distribution of disk-shaped GQDs. GQD-50 is found to be smaller compared to GQD-30 and GQD-110 (Fig. 2g–i), which is similar to the observations from TEM (Liu and Kim, 2015).

The XRD of the GQDs synthesized from various GO substrates has been represented in Fig. 3 a. GO shows a sharp peak at 2θ = 11.2° (Kumar et al., 2017) which were shifted to 2θ = 21.8°, corresponding to (002) plane, in case of GQD-50. In the case of GQD-30 and GQD-110 right shift of the peaks occurs indicating the presence of more graphite-like structure (Kumar et al., 2017). The d-spacing of GQD-50 (0.329 nm) as found from HRTEM image (Fig. S1) also corresponds to (002) plane, and hence, it is in agreement with the XRD plot. The broad peaks represent the reduced particle size (few nm) of the GQDs. The FTIR spectra of GO exhibit peaks at 1050 and 1410 cm−1 which are due to C–O–C stretching vibration of the alkoxy group and O–H bending vibration (Das et al., 2020). The GQDs synthesized at different conditions exhibit similar FTIR spectra (Fig. 3b). A broad peak was found at 3325 cm−1 which was due to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl group O–H. The presence of CC was reflected by the peak at 1639 cm−1 (Zhu et al., 2015). Fig. 3c represents Raman spectra of the synthesized GQDs, in the range of 900–3300 cm−1. The spectra of the GQDs are similar to graphene structure, despite their size reduction (Bharathi et al., 2017; Dervishi et al., 2019). The D band appears at ∼1315–1335 cm−1 due to edges and defects. In-plane C–C deformations gave rise to G-band at ∼1595 cm−1 (Lu et al., 2014). The D and G band intensity was found to be more in GQD-50 compared to others, which might be due to reduction in particle size as found from TEM images (Fig. 2d–f).

To analyze the presence of surface functional groups on synthesized GQDs, XPS spectra has been recorded (Fig. 4 ). The deconvoluted C1s spectra showed four surface components at binding energy 284.5 eV, 286.7 eV, 287.6 eV, and 289.1 eV which corresponds to sp2 C, sp3 C (C–OH), C–O–C as well as COOH functional groups, respectively. The presence of oxygen containing functional groups indicate the successful oxidation of graphite sheets into GQD. Deconvoluted O1s shows two major peaks at 530.7 eV and 531.4 eV which corresponds to CO, COOH, and C–O–C, respectively (Das et al., 2020; Hong et al., 2018). The atomic percentage of oxygen in GQD-50 is enhanced (about 61.21%) compared to other GQDs (Table S1).

The UV–visible absorption spectra (Fig. 5 a) of all the GQDs exhibit a peak at 230 nm, due to their typical π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 CC bonds (Paquin et al., 2015). The UV range absorption for GQD-110 was found to be higher compared to the other two GQDs. Fig. S4 shows that the bandgap, as calculated from Tauc's plot (Das et al., 2020), was narrower (4.2 eV) in the case of GQD-50 compared to GQD-30 (4.46 eV) and GQD-110 (4.5 eV). Fig. 5b-d exhibits excitation dependent PL spectra for all the synthesized GQDs dispersed in water at neutral pH. The excitation wavelength for the analysis was varied from 300 to 420 nm with an interval of 20 nm and the emission spectra was recorded. The effects of quantum confinement and surface traps are reported to be responsible for such behaviour (Dager et al., 2019). A redshift accompanied by a reduction in intensity occurs in the spectra as the excitation wavelength increases. GQD-50 showed the highest PL emission intensity at around 420 nm, upon excitation by 320 nm laser. The emission intensity at 420 nm was reduced in case of GQD-30 and GQD-110, depicting enhanced fluorescence of GQD-50 which might be attributed to their reduced particle size (Dager et al., 2019). GQD-110 on the other hand, exhibits a broad spectrum with two peaks centred at 426 and 542 nm with very less fluorescence. The optimization of GQD concentration and solution pH has also been studied to maximise the PL intensity. The PL intensity increases with increasing GQD-50 concentration from 0.1 to 0.5 mg/L (Fig. S5). However, the solution becomes unstable after 0.5 mg/L, with GQD depositing after a certain period of time. The pH study shows maximum PL intensity at pH of 7, while it decreases with increase/decrease of pH (Fig. S6). This concludes the solution concentration and pH should be maintained at 0.5 mg/L and 7, respectively, for further studies. The quantum yield obtained with QS as reference followed the order: GQD-50 (64.8%) > GQD-30 (60.8%) > GQD-110 (7.34%). The quantum yield of GQD-50 was higher than most of the studies reported (Deka et al., 2018; Salehnia et al., 2017). The variation in the structural and functional properties of GO resulted in the formation of GQDs of different sizes and hence different fluorescence properties.

The dynamics of recombination of the GQDs was analysed by performing the time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) analysis. The time resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curve (black line) and the values obtained have been represented in Fig. 6 (a-c) and Table S2, respectively. A triexponential function as described below (eq (2)) [32] is used for the decay curve fitting (red line).
(2)
Fit = A +
B 1
e
(
− t
τ 1
)
+
B 2
e
(
− t
τ 2
)
+
B 3
e
(
− t
τ 3
)
Where, τ and B denote the fluorescence lifetime and its corresponding amplitude, respectively. τ depends on the structural and functional characteristics of the QDs. The intrinsic states are associated with one τ, whereas the other two lifetimes arise due to the defect states. The intrinsic state corresponds to the exciton and localized states and exhibits a shorter recombination lifetime, compared to the defect states which are related to the presence of functional groups, interstitial atoms, and vacancies (Kang et al., 2019). The goodness of fit was determined by the χ2 which is expected to lie between 1.0 and 1.2. For our study, the measurements were performed at an emission wavelength of 420 nm and an excitation wavelength of 336 nm supplied by a diode laser.

All the types of synthesized GQDs display three lifetimes (τ) values (Table S2). The fluorescence emission due to the presence of oxygen rich functional groups in the surface leads to longer recombination lifetime values (
τ 2
,
τ 3
) compared to the intrinsic states (
τ 1
). In the present study, the magnitude and amplitude of
τ 1
for GOD-110 (0.76 ns; 50%) was found to be higher compared to GQD-30 (0.70 ns; 24%) and GQD-50 (0.67 ns; 25%). In the case of GQD-50, the defect state emissions i.e.
τ 2
(2.49 ns; 41%) and
τ 3
(8.93 ns; 34%) are found to be dominating, anticipating the presence of oxygen containing functional groups on the edges and surface in a higher proportion (Kang et al., 2019). Thus variation in the optoelectronic properties of the GQDs can be achieved by alteration of the initial GO structure.

The modified Stern-Volmer analysis (Eqs. (3) and (4)) was also performed to predict the quenching mechanism (Sinha et al., 2021). Interaction between electron rich GQD and electron deficient PA might result in complex formation between them.
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
(
K D
[ Q ]
)
e
( V
[ Q ]
)
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
Where,
F
and
F 0
are the fluorescence intensities of GQD in the presence and absence of PA, respectively; the Stern-Volmer dynamic and static quenching constants are represented by KD and V, respectively.
K q
is the quenching rate,
τ 0
is the average fluorescence lifetime without the presence of the quencher, and Q is the concentration of the quencher i.e. PA.

The plot (Fig. 7b) showed upward curvature which is the characteristic of dynamic quenching. This contradicts the findings of TRPL analysis which predicts static quenching as the dominant mechanism. Similar observations were reported for quenching of heavy metals by GQDs and other carbon materials (Chakraborti et al., 2013; Ciotta et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2014a, b). The studies concluded non-fluorescent complex formation although deviations from linearity were obtained in the Stern-Volmer plot. In the present case, the plot showed a good fit [R2 = 0.94] within the studied concentration range. Moreover, the values of KD and V as found from the modified SV equation (eq (3)) were found to be 1.1 × 10−5 and 6.05 × 103 L/mol, respectively, predicting static quenching as the dominant mechanism. Additionally, the value of V was greater than 200 L/mol predicting static quenching (Anusuya et al., 2021).
K q
was calculated to be 2.07 × 1012 L/mol sec.
The fluorophore-quencher binding parameters were calculated from eq. (5) as given below.
(5)
log
(
F 0
− F
F
)
= log K + nlog
[ Q ]

The number of binding sites (n) and the binding constant (K) can be calculated from the slope and intercept of the plot of
log
(
F 0
− F
F
)
versus log Q (Fig. S7), respectively. The plot showed a good linear fit (R2 = 0.98) and the values of n and K were found to be 1.3 and 6.02 × 104 L/mol. The limit of detection (LOD) was found from (Fig. S8) using the formula 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation and S is the slope of the linear fitted curve (Ma et al., 2019). Herein, LOD was estimated to be 1.2 μM (1.2 × 10−6 M). This depicts improved sensitivity of GQD-50 compared to the reported functionalized GQDs such as N-GQD (LOD - 8.13 × 10−5 M), Schiff-GQDs (LOD - 3.14 × 10−5 M) (Fan et al., 2021), or non-carbon compounds such as α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles (LOD – 3.3 μM) (Kumar et al., 2014) or Fe doped ZnO nanoellipsoids (2.9 μM) (Alaoui, 2009).

The formation of ground-state charge-transfer complex between PA and GQD-50 was further confirmed by UV–visible spectroscopy (Fig. S10). Significant changes in the spectra was observed for PA added GQD-50 compared to nascent GQD-50 solution. A new peak was observed at 350 nm, with reduced intensity compared to PA, depicting fluorophore-quencher complex formation. Similar findings were reported by other researchers [Roy et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014a, b].

Selectivity of the sensor is another important parameter in addition to sensitivity. In order to assess the selectivity of the GQD-50 sensor, the fluorescence lifetime analysis was performed in the presence of other aromatics such as toluene and nitrobenzene. The TRPL spectra (Fig. S9a,b) depicted negligible change in the fluorophore lifetime, concluding selectivity of GQD-50 towards PA detection.

In order to assess the effect of PA addition on the structural characteristics of GQD, the XRD of PA intercalated GQD-50 has been performed (Fig. S11). The plot does not show any significant changes compared to nascent GQD-50 (Fig. 3a). The peak corresponding to GQD was found at 2θ = 21.8°. This concludes that the addition of PA did not disrupt the structural features of GQD.

The study concludes that the same initial precursor i.e. GO with different surface and functional properties can result in the formation of GQD with varying size and optical properties. Hydrothermal reduction of GO synthesized at 50 °C resulted in the formation of GQD with improved properties compared to GO-30 and GO-110. This anticipates the carbon/oxygen ratio on the surface of the precursor affects the degree of reduction and hence the properties of the final product i.e. GQD. The synthesized GQD-50 can efficiently detect PA in water via fluorescence quenching unlike GQD-30, GQD-110, and other reported GQDs. The detection range varies up to 200 μM of PA. Stern-Volmer curve and the average lifetime obtained from fluorescence decay studies concludes static quenching as the dominant mechanism. This involves the formation of a complex between GQD and PA probably due to π-π interaction. The study opens a new platform to control the properties of GQD by tailoring the structure of GO and their application for sensing toxic water contaminants.

ce:para.ce:bold: Debarati Mukherjee: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Software, Writing – original draft. Pradip Das: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Software, Writing – original draft. Sukanya Kundu: Experimentation, Investigation, Validation, Writing- Editing. Bishnupada Mandal: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Thanking you, With warm regards,

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The characterization facilities provided by the facility of the central instrument (CIF), Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Guwahati are kindly acknowledged by the authors. The authors are thankful for the assistance with the fluorescence spectrophotometer provided by the Centre for Environment, IIT Guwahati.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, F., Gu, Z., Lei, W., Wang, W., Xia, X., Hao, Q., 2014a. Graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of copper(II) ions. Sensors Actuators, B Chem. 190, 516-522. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2013.09.009

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Wang et al., 2014b

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional graphene quantum dots for simultaneous targeted cellular imaging and drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, X., Sun, X., Lao, J., He, H., Cheng, T., Wang, M., Wang, S., Huang, F., 2014b. Multifunctional graphene quantum dots for simultaneous targeted cellular imaging and drug delivery. Colloids Surf. B., 122, 638-644. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2014.07.043

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Wollin and Dieter, 2005

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toxicological guidelines for monocyclic nitro-, amino- and aminonitroaromatics, nitramines, and nitrate esters in drinking water

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yan et al., 2010a

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Large, solution-processable graphene quantum dots as light absorbers for photovoltaics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yan, X., Cui, X., Li, B., Li, L.S., 2010a. Large, solution-processable graphene quantum dots as light absorbers for photovoltaics. Nano Lett. 10, 1869-1873. https://doi.org/10.1021/nl101060h

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yan et al., 2010b

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of large, stable colloidal graphene quantum dots with tunable size

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yan, X., Cui, X., Li, L.S., 2010b. Synthesis of large, stable colloidal graphene quantum dots with tunable size. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 132, 5944-5945. https://doi.org/10.1021/ja1009376

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhou et al., 2014

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel composite of graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step ultrasonic synthesis of graphene quantum dots with high quantum yield and their application in sensing alkaline phosphatase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, Y., Wang, G., Jiang, H., Chen, L., Zhang, X., 2015. One-step ultrasonic synthesis of graphene quantum dots with high quantum yield and their application in sensing alkaline phosphatase. Chem. Commun. 51, 948-951. https://doi.org/10.1039/c4cc07449a
